Analysis of s-alinines run during calibration of 2026 seal std

Peter Ditchfield

2026-03-26

Data used for the analyses

The data file used is

Salanine-for-R.csv”

This file is included in the folder with this r_markdown document but path names may change so beware.

It may be useful to have both the Name and Run columns as factors so they can be used for plotting

Figure 1a a basic cross plot

That is not great, the data looks to be systematically off from the ‘certified’ mean by about -0.1 per mil for carbon.

Calculate some basic stats for the measured Alanine’s

Mean value for \(\delta\) 13C is -27.18 ‰ (VPDB) with a standard deviation of 0.0593 ‰. The excepted value based on multiple years checking against international standards is -27.11‰ (VPDB). So there is an offset of ca.-0.07‰.

Mean value for \(\delta\) 15N is -1.569 ‰ (AIR) with a standard deviation of 0.136 ‰. The excepted value based on multiple years checking against international standards is -1.56‰ (AIR).

Ah No ! The alanine values used for diet are the 2022 calibration values which are \(\delta\) 13C = -27.18 ‰ (VPDB) with a standard deviation of 0.01 and \(\delta\) 15N = -1.51 ‰ (AIR) with a standard deviation of 0.01

So re plot with these values

Figure 1b a basic cross plot

That looks much better although the \(\delta\) 15N values look a bit skewed towards more negative values.

Figure 2a KDE plot of salanine \(\delta\) 13C data

Figure 2b a qqplot of the \(\delta\) 13C data

That looks as it should relatively normal with a mean at very close to -27.18.

Figure 3a KDE plot of salanine \(\delta\) 15N data

Figure 3b a qqplot of the \(\delta\) 15N data

This looks OK but not as good as the carbon data, with not as good a fit to the line of normality and with a mean of -1.569 ‰ that is somewhat displaced from the set value for \(\delta\) 15N of -1.50 ‰.

What about the other standards in these runs

Load other certified standards data file

It may be useful to have both the Name and Run columns as factors so they can be used for plotting

Basic cross plot

Note; only USGS40 and USGS88 have both carbon and nitrogen isotope data so these need to be separated out from the IAEA C6 for plotting. Note there are various data frames for various plots.

                      Now to make a cross plot
                      please do not ignore my indents


             \(\delta\) 13C VS \(\delta\) 15N Cross Plot for USGS40 and USGS88 Measured Values

Not bad reasonably tight clusters. Looking at the values USGS40 they are slightly over measured in the negative direction WRT both carbon and nitrogen. For USGS88 they are slightly over measured WRT nitrogen and slightly under WRT carbon. So not much scale compression for \(\delta\) 15N but some scale compression for \(\delta\) 13C at the less negative end i.e. as we move away from the \(\delta\) 13C value of the working standard alanine in the positive direction. This is why we do a scale correction.

calculate some basic stats for measured USGS 40

Figure 5a KDE plot of usgs40 \(\delta\) 13C data

So the mean measured \(\delta\) 13C is very close ca. + 0.03‰ compared to the certified \(\delta\) 13C value for USGS40.

Figure 5b KDE plot of usgs40 \(\delta\) 15N data

But the mean measured \(\delta\) 15N is ca. 0.32 ‰ too negative compared to the certified value that is quite a lot more of an off set than the \(\delta\) 13C data for USGS40.

Calculate some basic stats for measured USGS 88

Figure 6a KDE plot of usgs88 \(\delta\) 13C data

The mean measured \(\delta\) 13C is reasonably close (ca. + 0.2‰) compared to the certified \(\delta\) 13C value for USGS88.

Figure 6b KDE plot of usgs88 \(\delta\) 15N data

The mean measured \(\delta\) 15N for USGS88 is ca. 0.15 ‰ too positive compared to the certified value. However, given that the certified sd. is 0.14 and the measured sd. is 0.15 and that there is only 8 measured observations this is quite close.

Calculate some basic stats for measured IAEA C6 \(\delta\) 13C data

Figure 7a KDE plot of usgs88 \(\delta\) 13C data

The mean measured \(\delta\) 13C of -10.6 is reasonably close to the certified mean of -10.45 compared to the certified \(\delta\) 13C value for IAEA-C6 of -10.45, this ca. - 0.15‰ difference or under measurement of 13C maybe due to scale compression as this is ca. 16.7 ‰ away from the alanine working standard. However this value of -0.15‰ is less than the -0.21‰ value of the difference seen in the USGS88 data which is considerably closer to the the \(\delta\) 13C value of the alanine working standard.

Figure 8a plot of \(\delta\) 13C data for measured standards agains certified values

USGS40, which is very close to our alanine working standard sits on the 1:1 line. USGS88 and IAEAC6 which are further away from the working standard fall slightly below the 1:1 line and should be corrected for scale compression (under measurement).

So a one way stretch correction for \(\delta\) 13C measured value would be

The measured sample value + ((sample to alanine distance / C6 to alanine distance) + 0.15)

so as a test apply the above correction to the measured standards.

For mean IAEAC6

## [1] -10.45

for USGS88mean

## [1] -16.1713

What is needed is an average value of offset normalised for the distance from the working standard.

For USGS88 the distance from the working standard is (-16.06) - (-27.18) = 11.12.

the offset is 0.21‰

so the value for each per mil difference is 0.21/11.12 = 0.01888

For IAEAC6 the distance from the working standard is (-10.45) - (-27.18) = 16.73

the offset is 0.15

so the value for each per mil difference is 0.15/16.73 = 0.00896

the average is (0.01888+0.00896)/2 = 0.01392
